CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jul-02-2024 Continued To Dec-11-24 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar May-31-2024 Continued To Oct-25-24 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 302
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via Zoom:
Daniel L. Balsam, Esq., Tel. 415.869.2873 for plaintiff Alona Jennings, et al.;
Kavon Adli, Esq., Tel. 310.356.3257,for defendants THS Group, LLC and SPHW Company, Inc.

Hearing re: DEFENDANT SPHW COMPANY, INC.'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS.

The matter is contested. Argument is held. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling with the addition of the stay language reflecting that other discovery is stayed until the jurisdictional issue is decided.

Defendant SPHW Company, INC's motion to quash service of summons is continued to October 25, 2024, to allow for jurisdictional discovery.

Plaintiffs sued SPHW and others for violations Business & Professions Code 17529.5, which regulates spam sent to California residents. Plaintiffs allegedly received over 200 unsolicited commercial emails from third-party senders advertising SPHW's and other defendants' services. SPHW now moves to quash under CCP 418(a)(1) on grounds this court lacks personal jurisdiction over it.

Plaintiffs have not met their burden to demonstrate the court has general jurisdiction over SPHW. A court has general jurisdiction over defendants who are at home in the court's forum. (Farina v. SAVWCL III, LLC (2020) 50 Cal.App.5th 286, 294.) Here, plaintiffs provide no evidence that SPHW is incorporated or has its principal place of business in California. Even assuming the third-party senders' emails are attributable to SPHW, these emails fall short of the systematic and continuous contacts required to show SPHW is essentially at home in California.

Plaintiffs also fail to provide sufficient evidence for the court to find specific jurisdiction exists. It is undisputed that SPHW did not email plaintiffs directly. Thus, plaintiffs lack evidence that SPHW had direct contacts in California that satisfy the purposeful availment prong of the minimum contacts test. Alternatively, plaintiffs argue SPHW engaged third-party senders as their agents, and their agents' actions are attributable to them for jurisdictional purposes. (See Sher v. Johnson (9th Cir. 1990) 911 F.2d 1356, 1362 ["For purposes of personal jurisdiction, the actions of an agent are attributable to the principal"].) Plaintiffs, however, have not provided evidence sufficient to show an agency relationship, or that the alleged agents' actions suffice to confer jurisdiction over SPHW. (See Strasner v. Touchstone Wireless Repair & Logistics, LP (2016) 5 Cal.App.5th 215, 222 [requiring "specific evidentiary facts, through affidavits and other authenticated documents"].)

Plaintiffs are entitled to conduct jurisdictional discovery. Plaintiffs have leave to propound 20 special interrogatories; propound five requests for production of documents; and depose SPHW's person most knowledgeable for up to six hours, all regarding jurisdiction over SPHW.

Counsel for defendants to prepare and submit a new proposed order which includes the stay language and that complies with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Not Reported. Bailiff: Not Present. (301/RCE)

Hearing re: DEFENDANT THS GROUP, LLC'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS.

The matter is contested. Argument is held. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling with the addition of the stay language reflecting that other discovery is stayed until the jurisdictional issue is decided.

Defendant THS Group, LLC's motion to quash service of summons is continued to October 25, 2024, to allow for jurisdictional discovery.

Plaintiffs sued THS and others for violations Business & Professions Code 17529.5, which regulates spam sent to California residents. Plaintiffs allegedly received over 200 unsolicited commercial emails from third-party senders advertising THS's and other defendants' services. THS now moves to quash under CCP 418(a)(1) on grounds this court lacks personal jurisdiction over it.

Plaintiffs have not met their burden to demonstrate the court has general jurisdiction over THS. A court has general jurisdiction over defendants who are at home in the court's forum. (Farina v. SAVWCL III, LLC (2020) 50 Cal.App.5th 286, 294.) Here, plaintiffs provide no evidence that THS is incorporated or has its principal place of business in California. Even assuming the third-party senders' emails are attributable to THS, these emails fall short of the systematic and continuous contacts required to show THS is essentially at home in California.

Plaintiffs also fail to provide sufficient evidence for the court to find specific jurisdiction exists. It is undisputed that THS did not email plaintiffs directly. Thus, plaintiffs lack evidence that THS had direct contacts in California that satisfy the purposeful availment prong of the minimum contacts test. Plaintiffs, however, have not provided evidence sufficient to show an agency relationship, or that the alleged agents' actions suffice to confer jurisdiction over THS. (See Strasner v. Touchstone Wireless Repair & Logistics, LP (2016) 5 Cal.App.5th 215, 222 [requiring "specific evidentiary facts, through affidavits and other authenticated documents"].)

Plaintiffs are entitled to conduct jurisdictional discovery. Plaintiffs have leave to propound 20 special interrogatories; propound five requests for production of documents; and depose THS's person most knowledgeable for up to six hours, all regarding jurisdiction over THS.
